content.decision_question: Should Hungary implement individual proposals (T1, T2, or T3) incrementally despite the lens's caution that isolated fixes cannot resolve underlying equity and funding drivers, or should implementation be deferred/bundled until a more comprehensive reform package addressing funding formulas and church/state asymmetry is designed?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can document whether partial reforms produce measurable improvement even without addressing funding formulas and selection asymmetry, and can quantify the scale of unresolved disadvantage concentration. It cannot determine the normatively correct threshold of 'sufficient coordination' before action is justified, since this is a judgment about acceptable risk and sequencing rather than a fact to be measured.
content.tension: The Portuguese-reform lens repeatedly cautions that no isolated procedural, contractual, or structural fix (T1, T2, T3) can substitute for coordinated reform addressing funding formulas, teacher shortages, and the church/state selection asymmetry; yet pursuing a fully coordinated bundle is politically and administratively more demanding than adopting any single proposal, raising the question of whether partial reforms should proceed despite acknowledged insufficiency.
content.title: Coordinated structural reform versus isolated single-lever fixes
value_poles: avoiding false confidence in partial fixes
